diagnosis: Your tooth is severely damaged, decayed, or infected beyond repair and needs to be safely removed to protect your overall oral health.
treatment_steps: Take an X-ray to review the tooth shape, roots, and surrounding bone
Numb the area completely with local anesthesia
Gently loosen the tooth from its socket using a special instrument called an elevator
Remove the tooth using sterile forceps with a smooth, controlled motion
Clean the socket and remove any debris
Place sterile gauze over the extraction site and apply gentle pressure to control bleeding
Provide post-operative care instructions for healing
benefits: Removes the source of infection and pain
Prevents damage to nearby teeth and bone
Avoids the need for more extensive surgery later
risks: Normal bleeding, swelling, and discomfort for a few days
Temporary or, rarely, permanent numbness or tingling of the lip, chin, cheek, tongue, or gums due to nerve involvement
Dry socket (loss of the blood clot) causing delayed healing and pain
Damage to nearby teeth, fillings, or restorations
Jaw stiffness or soreness for several days
Sinus communication or opening for upper molars, which may require additional treatment to close
Need for additional treatment discovered during the procedure that may change the plan
Bleeding that lasts longer than expected
Allergic reaction to injections or medications
alternatives: No treatment at this time (the decay or infection will continue and may worsen)
Root canal treatment followed by a crown (if the tooth is salvageable)
Extraction with replacement options such as an implant, bridge, or partial denture to prevent neighboring teeth from shifting
risks_of_declining: If left untreated, the infection or decay will continue to worsen. This can lead to increased pain, abscess formation, spreading infection to surrounding tissues, and potential damage to adjacent teeth and bone.
prognosis: Healing typically takes about one to two weeks for the gum tissue. Bone takes longer to remodel. Most extractions heal without complications when post-operative instructions are followed. No outcome is guaranteed.
